Lower left: Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) }} {{-|left}} === History === Shrine tradition says it was founded in the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}} ({{jalink|97 BCE|Q41672}}-{{jalink|29 BCE|Q40835}}), and moved to the current place in the {{jalink|Tenpyo-shoho|Q1373169}} era ({{jalink|749|Q23624}}-{{jalink|756|Q31121}}). In the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small government-offering shrine. Under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine. There are three torii in the precinct, but in the {{jalink|2023|Q49622}} Okunoto earthquake of May, the first torii (about 9 m tall) collapsed and removal work began in August{{Cite web|和書|title=Removal begins at public expense for collapsed torii at Suzu Shrine|url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/articles/-/1155732 |website=Hokkoku Shimbun |date=2023-08-18 |access-date=2023-08-18 |language=ja}}. In the {{jalink|2024|Q49619}} {{jalink|Noto Peninsula earthquake (2024)|Q124060919|Noto Peninsula earthquake}}, the second torii collapsed due to quake and tsunami effects, and the third torii developed cracks{{Cite web |title=Suzu's Suzu Shrine heavily damaged again after last year; assistant priest Toyonobu Sarume: "Only rebuilding remains"|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/general/nikkan/news/202401210000971.html |website=Nikkan Sports|date=2024-01-21 |access-date=2024-01-21 |language=ja}}. In the same quake, the temizuya was completely destroyed, doors of the main hall were warped, and stone lanterns and guardian dogs were also damaged. === Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property * Five seated wooden male deity statues ; Natural Monument * Suzu Shrine sacred grove File:須須神社 - panoramio.jpg|Suzu Shrine sacred grove === Access === * By car : From {{jalink|Noto Satoyama Kaido|Q11612031}}{{jalink|Anamizu Interchange|Q11597233|Anamizu I.C.}}, go east via {{jalink|Suzu Road|Q11573344}}, then from the Rice Center-mae intersection take Prefectural Route 287 for about 1 hour. * Public transport : {{jalink|Suzu Bus}}, Noro-Iida (sea) route bound for "Noro," get off at "Suzu Jinja-mae"{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.suzu.lg.jp/site/kankou/1530.html |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Suzu City |date=2022-01-17 |accessdate=2024-01-12 }}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === {{参照方法|section=1|date=2024-01}} * Precinct signboard "Suzu Shrine <>" (no author/date listed) * ''Ishikawa Prefecture Shrine Record,'' edited and published by Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines (Oct 26, 1976) * ''Kano Local Terms,'' by Ken Hiki (published by {{jalink|Hokkoku Shimbun|Q11401312|Hokkoku Shimbun}} on Aug 1, 1956) === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{jalink|Noto Province|Q1047671}} * {{jalink|Okunoto|Q11446096}} * {{jalink|Suzu Cape|Q11610957}} * {{jalink|Suzu District|Q7651143}} * {{jalink|Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314}} * {{jalink|Heki Village (Ishikawa)|Q17174281}} * {{jalink|Saikai Village (Suzu District, Ishikawa)|Q11629087}} === External links === * [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ Suzu Shrine] / [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ Suzu Shrine Okumiya] - Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines * [https://www.city.suzu.lg.jp/kankounavi/spot/history/temple/suzu_shrine.html Suzu Shrine] - Suzu Tourism Navi Net (Suzu City) * [http://www.pref.ishikawa.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunkazai/siseki/3-5.html#susu Suzu Shrine Sacred Grove] - revision.comment: 1 revision imported: used local AI agents to translate these pages. Contrary to the description it was actually Claude Code for a few, OpenClaw with Codex, and regular Codex. Ollama and DeepSeek were too bad to actually use. see categorization since some of them are very questionable, especially if there was a massive decrease in page size or the {{jawiki| template is minimal or absent on the page

link|Q11664846}} == Japanese content == {{右| {{神社 |名称 = Suzu Shrine |画像 = [[ファイル:Suzujinjahonden.JPG|250px|Takakura-gu main hall]]
Takakura-gu main hall |所在地 = Misaki-cho Jike 4-2, Suzu, Ishikawa{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|29|54|N|137|20|48|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine}}| |祭神 = Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto, Miho Susumi no Mikoto, Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto, and 3 others |社格 = Shikinaisha, Prefectural Shrine |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}} ({{jalink|97 BCE|Q41672}}-{{jalink|29 BCE|Q40835}}) |本殿 = nagare-zukuri |別名 = |例祭 = {{jalink|September 14|Q2847}} |神事 = }} {{神社 |名称 = Suzu Shrine Okumiya |画像 = [[ファイル:Suzujinjaokumiyairiguchi.JPG|250px|Okumiya entrance]]
Okumiya entrance |所在地 = Noro-machi Ka-74, Suzu{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ |title=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|30|42.1|N|137|19|48.5|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine Okumiya}}| |祭神 = Mihosusumi-no-Mikoto, {{jalink|Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Futsunushi|Q1107286|Futsunushi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = |創建 = (trad.) reign of Emperor Sujin |本殿 = |別名 = Suzugatake Oku Shrine, Suzuoku Daimyojin |例祭 = |神事 = }} }} '''Suzu Shrine''' (すずじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Suzu|Q846345}}, {{jalink|Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281}}. '''Takakura-gu''' and '''Kinbun-gu''' are in {{jalink|Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|Misaki-cho Jike}}, Suzu, and the '''Okumiya''' is in Noro-machi, Suzu. === Overview === It is located in the northeastern part of Suzu City. The enshrined deities are {{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}, Miho Susumi-no-Mikoto, {{jalink|Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and three others. Near Takakura-gu are Suzu Shrine Okumiya and Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu. It holds cultural properties including a wooden male deity statue designated as a national {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}. On {{jalink|April 24, 2015|Q2529}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 27), it was certified as a {{jalink|Japan Heritage|Q18382798}} component under "Lanterns Dancing on the Peninsula: Noto - Enthralling Kiriko Festivals." {{maplink2 |frame=yes |zoom=12 |frame-lat=37.48 |frame-long=137.31 |frame-width=300 |frame-height=400 |frame-align=left |type=point |marker=1 |title=Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu |coord={{Coord |37.4982134 |137.3461987 }} |type2=point |marker2=2 |title2=Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu |coord2={{Coord |37.495727 |137.3445761 }} |type3=point |marker3=3 |title3=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |coord3={{Coord |37.5116835 |137.3301375 }} |type4=point |marker4=bus |title4=Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) |coord4={{Coord |37.4414395 |137.2701412 }} |text=(1) Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu
(2) Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu
(3) Suzu Shrine Okumiya
Lower left: Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) }} {{-|left}} === History === Shrine tradition says it was founded in the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}} ({{jalink|97 BCE|Q41672}}-{{jalink|29 BCE|Q40835}}), and moved to the current place in the {{jalink|Tenpyo-shoho|Q1373169}} era ({{jalink|749|Q23624}}-{{jalink|756|Q31121}}). In the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small government-offering shrine. Under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine. There are three torii in the precinct, but in the {{jalink|2023|Q49622}} Okunoto earthquake of May, the first torii (about 9 m tall) collapsed and removal work began in August{{Cite web|和書|title=Removal begins at public expense for collapsed torii at Suzu Shrine|url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/articles/-/1155732 |website=Hokkoku Shimbun |date=2023-08-18 |access-date=2023-08-18 |language=ja}}. In the {{jalink|2024|Q49619}} {{jalink|Noto Peninsula earthquake (2024)|Q124060919|Noto Peninsula earthquake}}, the second torii collapsed due to quake and tsunami effects, and the third torii developed cracks{{Cite web |title=Suzu's Suzu Shrine heavily damaged again after last year; assistant priest Toyonobu Sarume: "Only rebuilding remains"|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/general/nikkan/news/202401210000971.html |website=Nikkan Sports|date=2024-01-21 |access-date=2024-01-21 |language=ja}}. In the same quake, the temizuya was completely destroyed, doors of the main hall were warped, and stone lanterns and guardian dogs were also damaged. === Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property * Five seated wooden male deity statues ; Natural Monument * Suzu Shrine sacred grove File:須須神社 - panoramio.jpg|Suzu Shrine sacred grove === Access === * By car : From {{jalink|Noto Satoyama Kaido|Q11612031}}{{jalink|Anamizu Interchange|Q11597233|Anamizu I.C.}}, go east via {{jalink|Suzu Road|Q11573344}}, then from the Rice Center-mae intersection take Prefectural Route 287 for about 1 hour. * Public transport : {{jalink|Suzu Bus}}, Noro-Iida (sea) route bound for "Noro," get off at "Suzu Jinja-mae"{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.suzu.lg.jp/site/kankou/1530.html |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Suzu City |date=2022-01-17 |accessdate=2024-01-12 }}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === {{参照方法|section=1|date=2024-01}} * Precinct signboard "Suzu Shrine <>" (no author/date listed) * ''Ishikawa Prefecture Shrine Record,'' edited and published by Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines (Oct 26, 1976) * ''Kano Local Terms,'' by Ken Hiki (published by {{jalink|Hokkoku Shimbun|Q11401312|Hokkoku Shimbun}} on Aug 1, 1956) === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{jalink|Noto Province|Q1047671}} * {{jalink|Okunoto|Q11446096}} * {{jalink|Suzu Cape|Q11610957}} * {{jalink|Suzu District|Q7651143}} * {{jalink|Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314}} * {{jalink|Heki Village (Ishikawa)|Q17174281}} * {{jalink|Saikai Village (Suzu District, Ishikawa)|Q11629087}} === External links === * [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ Suzu Shrine] / [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ Suzu Shrine Okumiya] - Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines * [https://www.city.suzu.lg.jp/kankounavi/spot/history/temple/suzu_shrine.html Suzu Shrine] - Suzu Tourism Navi Net (Suzu City) * [http://www.pref.ishikawa.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunkazai/siseki/3-5.html#susu Suzu Shrine Sacred Grove] - Ishikawa Prefectural Board of Education {{DEFAULTSORT:すすしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Suzu Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Suzu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Suzu Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Noto Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:石川県の神社]] [[Category:石川県の旧県社]] [[Category:能登国の式内社]] [[Category:石川県の重要文化財]] [[Category:珠洲市の建築物]] [[Category:珠洲市の歴史]] [[Category:日本遺産]] [[Category:need translation]]

Takakura-gu main hall |所在地 = Misaki-cho Jike 4-2, Suzu, Ishikawa{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|29|54|N|137|20|48|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine}}| |祭神 = Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto, Miho Susumi no Mikoto, Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto, and 3 others |社格 = Shikinaisha, Prefectural Shrine |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}} ({{jalink|97 BCE|Q41672}}-{{jalink|29 BCE|Q40835}}) |本殿 = nagare-zukuri |別名 = |例祭 = {{jalink|September 14|Q2847}} |神事 = }} {{神社 |名称 = Suzu Shrine Okumiya |画像 = [[ファイル:Suzujinjaokumiyairiguchi.JPG|250px|Okumiya entrance]]
Okumiya entrance |所在地 = Noro-machi Ka-74, Suzu{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ |title=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|30|42.1|N|137|19|48.5|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine Okumiya}}| |祭神 = Mihosusumi-no-Mikoto, {{jalink|Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Futsunushi|Q1107286|Futsunushi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = |創建 = (trad.) reign of Emperor Sujin |本殿 = |別名 = Suzugatake Oku Shrine, Suzuoku Daimyojin |例祭 = |神事 = }} }} '''Suzu Shrine''' (すずじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Suzu|Q846345}}, {{jalink|Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281}}. '''Takakura-gu''' and '''Kinbun-gu''' are in {{jalink|Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|Misaki-cho Jike}}, Suzu, and the '''Okumiya''' is in Noro-machi, Suzu. === Overview === It is located in the northeastern part of Suzu City. The enshrined deities are {{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}, Miho Susumi-no-Mikoto, {{jalink|Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and three others. Near Takakura-gu are Suzu Shrine Okumiya and Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu. It holds cultural properties including a wooden male deity statue designated as a national {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}. On {{jalink|April 24, 2015|Q2529}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 27), it was certified as a {{jalink|Japan Heritage|Q18382798}} component under "Lanterns Dancing on the Peninsula: Noto - Enthralling Kiriko Festivals." {{maplink2 |frame=yes |zoom=12 |frame-lat=37.48 |frame-long=137.31 |frame-width=300 |frame-height=400 |frame-align=left |type=point |marker=1 |title=Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu |coord={{Coord |37.4982134 |137.3461987 }} |type2=point |marker2=2 |title2=Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu |coord2={{Coord |37.495727 |137.3445761 }} |type3=point |marker3=3 |title3=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |coord3={{Coord |37.5116835 |137.3301375 }} |type4=point |marker4=bus |title4=Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) |coord4={{Coord |37.4414395 |137.2701412 }} |text=(1) Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu
(2) Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu
(3) Suzu Shrine Okumiya
Lower left: Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) }} {{-|left}} === History === Shrine tradition says it was founded in the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}} ({{jalink|97 BCE|Q41672}}-{{jalink|29 BCE|Q40835}}), and moved to the current place in the {{jalink|Tenpyo-shoho|Q1373169}} era ({{jalink|749|Q23624}}-{{jalink|756|Q31121}}). In the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small government-offering shrine. Under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine. There are three torii in the precinct, but in the {{jalink|2023|Q49622}} Okunoto earthquake of May, the first torii (about 9 m tall) collapsed and removal work began in August{{Cite web|和書|title=Removal begins at public expense for collapsed torii at Suzu Shrine|url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/articles/-/1155732 |website=Hokkoku Shimbun |date=2023-08-18 |access-date=2023-08-18 |language=ja}}. In the {{jalink|2024|Q49619}} {{jalink|Noto Peninsula earthquake (2024)|Q124060919|Noto Peninsula earthquake}}, the second torii collapsed due to quake and tsunami effects, and the third torii developed cracks{{Cite web |title=Suzu's Suzu Shrine heavily damaged again after last year; assistant priest Toyonobu Sarume: "Only rebuilding remains"|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/general/nikkan/news/202401210000971.html |website=Nikkan Sports|date=2024-01-21 |access-date=2024-01-21 |language=ja}}. In the same quake, the temizuya was completely destroyed, doors of the main hall were warped, and stone lanterns and guardian dogs were also damaged. === Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property * Five seated wooden male deity statues ; Natural Monument * Suzu Shrine sacred grove File:須須神社 - panoramio.jpg|Suzu Shrine sacred grove === Access === * By car : From {{jalink|Noto Satoyama Kaido|Q11612031}}{{jalink|Anamizu Interchange|Q11597233|Anamizu I.C.}}, go east via {{jalink|Suzu Road|Q11573344}}, then from the Rice Center-mae intersection take Prefectural Route 287 for about 1 hour. * Public transport : {{jalink|Suzu Bus}}, Noro-Iida (sea) route bound for "Noro," get off at "Suzu Jinja-mae"{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.suzu.lg.jp/site/kankou/1530.html |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Suzu City |date=2022-01-17 |accessdate=2024-01-12 }}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === {{参照方法|section=1|date=2024-01}} * Precinct signboard "Suzu Shrine <>" (no author/date listed) * ''Ishikawa Prefecture Shrine Record,'' edited and published by Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines (Oct 26, 1976) * ''Kano Local Terms,'' by Ken Hiki (published by {{jalink|Hokkoku Shimbun|Q11401312|Hokkoku Shimbun}} on Aug 1, 1956) === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{jalink|Noto Province|Q1047671}} * {{jalink|Okunoto|Q11446096}} * {{jalink|Suzu Cape|Q11610957}} * {{jalink|Suzu District|Q7651143}} * {{jalink|Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314}} * {{jalink|Heki Village (Ishikawa)|Q17174281}} * {{jalink|Saikai Village (Suzu District, Ishikawa)|Q11629087}} === External links === * [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ Suzu Shrine] / [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ Suzu Shrine Okumiya] - '''Takakura-gu''' and '''Kinbun-gu''' are in {{jalink|Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|Misaki-cho Jike}}, Suzu, and the '''Okumiya''' is in Noro-machi, Suzu. === Overview === It is located in the northeastern part of Suzu City. The enshrined deities are {{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}, Miho Susumi-no-Mikoto, {{jalink|Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and three others. Near Takakura-gu are Suzu Shrine Okumiya and Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu. It holds cultural properties including a wooden male deity statue designated as a national {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}. On {{jalink|April 24, 2015|Q2529}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 27), it was certified as a {{jalink|Japan Heritage|Q18382798}} component under "Lanterns Dancing on the Peninsula: Noto - Enthralling Kiriko Festivals." {{maplink2 |frame=yes |zoom=12 |frame-lat=37.48 |frame-long=137.31 |frame-width=300 |frame-height=400 |frame-align=left |type=point |marker=1 |title=Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu |coord={{Coord |37.4982134 |137.3461987 }} |type2=point |marker2=2 |title2=Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu |coord2={{Coord |37.495727 |137.3445761 }} |type3=point |marker3=3 |title3=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |coord3={{Coord |37.5116835 |137.3301375 }} |type4=point |marker4=bus |title4=Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) |coord4={{Coord |37.4414395 |137.2701412 }} |text=(1) Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu

Lower left: Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) }} {{-|left}} === History === Shrine tradition says it was founded in the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}} ({{jalink|97 BCE|Q41672}}-{{jalink|29 BCE|Q40835}}), and moved to the current place in the {{jalink|Tenpyo-shoho|Q1373169}} era ({{jalink|749|Q23624}}-{{jalink|756|Q31121}}). In the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small government-offering shrine. Under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine. There are three torii in the precinct, but in the {{jalink|2023|Q49622}} Okunoto earthquake of May, the first torii (about 9 m tall) collapsed and removal work began in August{{Cite web|和書|title=Removal begins at public expense for collapsed torii at Suzu Shrine|url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/articles/-/1155732 |website=Hokkoku Shimbun |date=2023-08-18 |access-date=2023-08-18 |language=ja}}. In the {{jalink|2024|Q49619}} {{jalink|Noto Peninsula earthquake (2024)|Q124060919|Noto Peninsula earthquake}}, the second torii collapsed due to quake and tsunami effects, and the third torii developed cracks{{Cite web |title=Suzu's Suzu Shrine heavily damaged again after last year; assistant priest Toyonobu Sarume: "Only rebuilding remains"|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/general/nikkan/news/202401210000971.html |website=Nikkan Sports|date=2024-01-21 |access-date=2024-01-21 |language=ja}}. In the same quake, the temizuya was completely destroyed, doors of the main hall were warped, and stone lanterns and guardian dogs were also damaged. === Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property * Five seated wooden male deity statues ; Natural Monument * Suzu Shrine sacred grove File:須須神社 - Takakura-gu main hall |所在地 = Misaki-cho Jike 4-2, Suzu, Ishikawa{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|29|54|N|137|20|48|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine}}| |祭神 = Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto, Miho Susumi no Mikoto, Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto, and 3 others |社格 = Shikinaisha, Prefectural Shrine |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}} ({{jalink|97 BCE|Q41672}}-{{jalink|29 BCE|Q40835}}) |本殿 = nagare-zukuri |別名 = |例祭 = {{jalink|September 14|Q2847}} |神事 = }} {{神社 |名称 = Suzu Shrine Okumiya |画像 = [[ファイル:Suzujinjaokumiyairiguchi.JPG|250px|Okumiya entrance]]
Okumiya entrance |所在地 = Noro-machi Ka-74, Suzu{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ |title=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|30|42.1|N|137|19|48.5|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine Okumiya}}| |祭神 = Mihosusumi-no-Mikoto, {{jalink|Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Futsunushi|Q1107286|Futsunushi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = |創建 = (trad.) reign of Emperor Sujin |本殿 = |別名 = Suzugatake Oku Shrine, Suzuoku Daimyojin |例祭 = |神事 = }} }} '''Suzu Shrine''' (すずじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Suzu|Q846345}}, {{jalink|Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281}}. '''Takakura-gu''' and '''Kinbun-gu''' are in {{jalink|Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|Misaki-cho Jike}}, Suzu, and the '''Okumiya''' is in Noro-machi, Suzu. === Overview === It is located in the northeastern part of Suzu City. The enshrined deities are {{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}, Miho Susumi-no-Mikoto, {{jalink|Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and three others. Near Takakura-gu are Suzu Shrine Okumiya and Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu. It holds cultural properties including a wooden male deity statue designated as a national {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}. On {{jalink|April 24, 2015|Q2529}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 27), it was certified as a {{jalink|Japan Heritage|Q18382798}} component under "Lanterns Dancing on the Peninsula: Noto - Enthralling Kiriko Festivals." {{maplink2 |frame=yes |zoom=12 |frame-lat=37.48 |frame-long=137.31 |frame-width=300 |frame-height=400 |frame-align=left |type=point |marker=1 |title=Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu |coord={{Coord |37.4982134 |137.3461987 }} |type2=point |marker2=2 |title2=Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu |coord2={{Coord |37.495727 |137.3445761 }} |type3=point |marker3=3 |title3=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |coord3={{Coord |37.5116835 |137.3301375 }} |type4=point |marker4=bus |title4=Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) |coord4={{Coord |37.4414395 |137.2701412 }} |text=(1) Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu
(2) Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu
(3) Suzu Shrine Okumiya
Lower left: Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) }} {{-|left}} === History === Shrine tradition says it was founded in the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}} ({{jalink|97 BCE|Q41672}}-{{jalink|29 BCE|Q40835}}), and moved to the current place in the {{jalink|Tenpyo-shoho|Q1373169}} era ({{jalink|749|Q23624}}-{{jalink|756|Q31121}}). In the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small government-offering shrine. Under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine. There are three torii in the precinct, but in the {{jalink|2023|Q49622}} Okunoto earthquake of May, the first torii (about 9 m tall) collapsed and removal work began in August{{Cite web|和書|title=Removal begins at public expense for collapsed torii at Suzu Shrine|url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/articles/-/1155732 |website=Hokkoku Shimbun |date=2023-08-18 |access-date=2023-08-18 |language=ja}}. In the {{jalink|2024|Q49619}} {{jalink|Noto Peninsula earthquake (2024)|Q124060919|Noto Peninsula earthquake}}, the second torii collapsed due to quake and tsunami effects, and the third torii developed cracks{{Cite web |title=Suzu's Suzu Shrine heavily damaged again after last year; assistant priest Toyonobu Sarume: "Only rebuilding remains"|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/general/nikkan/news/202401210000971.html |website=Nikkan Sports|date=2024-01-21 |access-date=2024-01-21 |language=ja}}. In the same quake, the temizuya was completely destroyed, doors of the main hall were warped, and stone lanterns and guardian dogs were also damaged. === Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property * Five seated wooden male deity statues ; Natural Monument * Suzu Shrine sacred grove File:須須神社 - panoramio.jpg|Suzu Shrine sacred grove === Access === * By car : From {{jalink|Noto Satoyama Kaido|Q11612031}}{{jalink|Anamizu Interchange|Q11597233|Anamizu I.C.}}, go east via {{jalink|Suzu Road|Q11573344}}, then from the Rice Center-mae intersection take Prefectural Route 287 for about 1 hour. * Public transport : {{jalink|Suzu Bus}}, Noro-Iida (sea) route bound for "Noro," get off at "Suzu Jinja-mae"{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.suzu.lg.jp/site/kankou/1530.html |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Suzu City |date=2022-01-17 |accessdate=2024-01-12 }}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === {{参照方法|section=1|date=2024-01}} * Precinct signboard "Suzu Shrine <>" (no author/date listed) * ''Ishikawa Prefecture Shrine Record,'' edited and published by Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines (Oct 26, 1976) * ''Kano Local Terms,'' by Ken Hiki (published by {{jalink|Hokkoku Shimbun|Q11401312|Hokkoku Shimbun}} on Aug 1, 1956) === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{jalink|Noto Province|Q1047671}} * {{jalink|Okunoto|Q11446096}} * {{jalink|Suzu Cape|Q11610957}} * {{jalink|Suzu District|Q7651143}} * {{jalink|Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314}} * {{jalink|Heki Village (Ishikawa)|Q17174281}} * {{jalink|Saikai Village (Suzu District, Ishikawa)|Q11629087}} === External links === * [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ Suzu Shrine] / [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ Suzu Shrine Okumiya] - '''Takakura-gu''' and '''Kinbun-gu''' are in {{jalink|Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|Misaki-cho Jike}}, Suzu, and the '''Okumiya''' is in Noro-machi, Suzu. === Overview === It is located in the northeastern part of Suzu City. The enshrined deities are {{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}, Miho Susumi-no-Mikoto, {{jalink|Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and three others. Near Takakura-gu are Suzu Shrine Okumiya and Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu. It holds cultural properties including a wooden male deity statue designated as a national {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}. On {{jalink|April 24, 2015|Q2529}} ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 27), it was certified as a {{jalink|Japan Heritage|Q18382798}} component under "Lanterns Dancing on the Peninsula: Noto - Enthralling Kiriko Festivals." {{maplink2 |frame=yes |zoom=12 |frame-lat=37.48 |frame-long=137.31 |frame-width=300 |frame-height=400 |frame-align=left |type=point |marker=1 |title=Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu |coord={{Coord |37.4982134 |137.3461987 }} |type2=point |marker2=2 |title2=Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu |coord2={{Coord |37.495727 |137.3445761 }} |type3=point |marker3=3 |title3=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |coord3={{Coord |37.5116835 |137.3301375 }} |type4=point |marker4=bus |title4=Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) |coord4={{Coord |37.4414395 |137.2701412 }} |text=(1) Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu

Lower left: Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) }} {{-|left}} === History === Shrine tradition says it was founded in the reign of {{jalink|Emperor Sujin|Q333530}} ({{jalink|97 BCE|Q41672}}-{{jalink|29 BCE|Q40835}}), and moved to the current place in the {{jalink|Tenpyo-shoho|Q1373169}} era ({{jalink|749|Q23624}}-{{jalink|756|Q31121}}). In the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small government-offering shrine. Under the {{jalink|modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine. There are three torii in the precinct, but in the {{jalink|2023|Q49622}} Okunoto earthquake of May, the first torii (about 9 m tall) collapsed and removal work began in August{{Cite web|和書|title=Removal begins at public expense for collapsed torii at Suzu Shrine|url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/articles/-/1155732 |website=Hokkoku Shimbun |date=2023-08-18 |access-date=2023-08-18 |language=ja}}. In the {{jalink|2024|Q49619}} {{jalink|Noto Peninsula earthquake (2024)|Q124060919|Noto Peninsula earthquake}}, the second torii collapsed due to quake and tsunami effects, and the third torii developed cracks{{Cite web |title=Suzu's Suzu Shrine heavily damaged again after last year; assistant priest Toyonobu Sarume: "Only rebuilding remains"|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/general/nikkan/news/202401210000971.html |website=Nikkan Sports|date=2024-01-21 |access-date=2024-01-21 |language=ja}}. In the same quake, the temizuya was completely destroyed, doors of the main hall were warped, and stone lanterns and guardian dogs were also damaged. === Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property * Five seated wooden male deity statues ; Natural Monument * Suzu Shrine sacred grove File:須須神社 - panoramio.jpg|Suzu Shrine sacred grove === Access === * By car : From {{jalink|Noto Satoyama Kaido|Q11612031}}{{jalink|Anamizu Interchange|Q11597233|Anamizu I.C.}}, go east via {{jalink|Suzu Road|Q11573344}}, then from the Rice Center-mae intersection take Prefectural Route 287 for about 1 hour. * Public transport : {{jalink|Suzu Bus}}, Noro-Iida (sea) route bound for "Noro," get off at "Suzu Jinja-mae"{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.suzu.lg.jp/site/kankou/1530.html |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Suzu City |date=2022-01-17 |accessdate=2024-01-12 }}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === {{参照方法|section=1|date=2024-01}} * Precinct signboard "Suzu Shrine <>" (no author/date listed) * ''Ishikawa Prefecture Shrine Record,'' edited and published by Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines (Oct 26, 1976) * ''Kano Local Terms,'' by Ken Hiki (published by {{jalink|Hokkoku Shimbun|Q11401312|Hokkoku Shimbun}} on Aug 1, 1956) === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{jalink|Noto Province|Q1047671}} * {{jalink|Okunoto|Q11446096}} * {{jalink|Suzu Cape|Q11610957}} * {{jalink|Suzu District|Q7651143}} * {{jalink|Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314}} * {{jalink|Heki Village (Ishikawa)|Q17174281}} * {{jalink|Saikai Village (Suzu District, Ishikawa)|Q11629087}} === External links === * [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ Suzu Shrine] / [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ Suzu Shrine Okumiya] - Okumiya entrance |所在地 = Noro-machi Ka-74, Suzu{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ |title=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|30|42.1|N|137|19|48.5|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine Okumiya}}| |祭神 = Mihosusumi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Futsunushi|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = |創建 = (trad.) reign of Emperor Sujin |本殿 = |別名 = Suzugatake Oku Shrine, Suzuoku Daimyojin |例祭 = |神事 = }} }} '''Suzu Shrine''' (すずじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Suzu|Q846345|WD=Q846345}}, {{ill|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281|WD=Q131281}}. '''Takakura-gu''' and '''Kinbun-gu''' are in {{ill|lt=Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|WD=Q11355314|lt=Misaki-cho Jike}}, Suzu, and the '''Okumiya''' is in Noro-machi, Suzu. === Overview === It is located in the northeastern part of Suzu City. The enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}, Miho Susumi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and three others. Near Takakura-gu are Suzu Shrine Okumiya and Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu. It holds cultural properties including a wooden male deity statue designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}. On {{ill|lt=April 24, 2015|Q2529|WD=Q2529}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 27), it was certified as a {{ill|lt=Japan Heritage|Q18382798|WD=Q18382798}} component under "Lanterns Dancing on the Peninsula: Noto - Enthralling Kiriko Festivals." {{maplink2 |frame=yes |zoom=12 |frame-lat=37.48 |frame-long=137.31 |frame-width=300 |frame-height=400 |frame-align=left |type=point |marker=1 |title=Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu |coord={{Coord |37.4982134 |137.3461987 }} |type2=point |marker2=2 |title2=Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu |coord2={{Coord |37.495727 |137.3445761 }} |type3=point |marker3=3 |title3=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |coord3={{Coord |37.5116835 |137.3301375 }} |type4=point |marker4=bus |title4=Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) |coord4={{Coord |37.4414395 |137.2701412 }} |text=(1) Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu
(2) Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu
(3) Suzu Shrine Okumiya
Lower left: Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) }} {{-|left}} === History === Shrine tradition says it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}} ({{ill|lt=97 BCE|Q41672|WD=Q41672}}-{{ill|lt=29 BCE|Q40835|WD=Q40835}}), and moved to the current place in the {{ill|lt=Tenpyo-shoho|Q1373169|WD=Q1373169}} era ({{ill|lt=749|Q23624|WD=Q23624}}-{{ill|lt=756|Q31121|WD=Q31121}}). In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small government-offering shrine. Under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine. There are three torii in the precinct, but in the {{ill|lt=2023|Q49622|WD=Q49622}} Okunoto earthquake of May, the first torii (about 9 m tall) collapsed and removal work began in August{{Cite web|和書|title=Removal begins at public expense for collapsed torii at Suzu Shrine|url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/articles/-/1155732 |website=Hokkoku Shimbun |date=2023-08-18 |access-date=2023-08-18 |language=ja}}. In the {{ill|lt=2024|Q49619|WD=Q49619}} {{ill|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake (2024)|Q124060919|WD=Q124060919|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake}}, the second torii collapsed due to quake and tsunami effects, and the third torii developed cracks{{Cite web |title=Suzu's Suzu Shrine heavily damaged again after last year; assistant priest Toyonobu Sarume: "Only rebuilding remains"|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/general/nikkan/news/202401210000971.html |website=Nikkan Sports|date=2024-01-21 |access-date=2024-01-21 |language=ja}}. In the same quake, the temizuya was completely destroyed, doors of the main hall were warped, and stone lanterns and guardian dogs were also damaged. === Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property * Five seated wooden male deity statues ; Natural Monument * Suzu Shrine sacred grove File:須須神社 - panoramio.jpg|Suzu Shrine sacred grove === Access === * By car : From {{ill|lt=Noto Satoyama Kaido|Q11612031|WD=Q11612031}}{{ill|lt=Anamizu Interchange|Q11597233|WD=Q11597233|lt=Anamizu I.C.}}, go east via {{ill|lt=Suzu Road|Q11573344|WD=Q11573344}}, then from the Rice Center-mae intersection take Prefectural Route 287 for about 1 hour. * Public transport : {{ill||lt=Suzu Bus|UNKNOWN}}, Noro-Iida (sea) route bound for "Noro," get off at "Suzu Jinja-mae"{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.suzu.lg.jp/site/kankou/1530.html |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Suzu City |date=2022-01-17 |accessdate=2024-01-12 }}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === {{参照方法|section=1|date=2024-01}} * Precinct signboard "Suzu Shrine <>" (no author/date listed) * ''Ishikawa Prefecture Shrine Record,'' edited and published by Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines (Oct 26, 1976) * ''Kano Local Terms,'' by Ken Hiki (published by {{ill|lt=Hokkoku Shimbun|Q11401312|WD=Q11401312|lt=Hokkoku Shimbun}} on Aug 1, 1956) === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|lt=Noto Province|Q1047671|WD=Q1047671}} * {{ill|lt=Okunoto|Q11446096|WD=Q11446096}} * {{ill|lt=Suzu Cape|Q11610957|WD=Q11610957}} * {{ill|lt=Suzu District|Q7651143|WD=Q7651143}} * {{ill|lt=Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|WD=Q11355314}} * {{ill|lt=Heki Village (Ishikawa)|Q17174281|WD=Q17174281}} * {{ill|lt=Saikai Village (Suzu District, Ishikawa)|Q11629087|WD=Q11629087}} === External links === * [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ Suzu Shrine] / [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ Suzu Shrine Okumiya] - Takakura-gu main hall |所在地 = Misaki-cho Jike 4-2, Suzu, Ishikawa{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|29|54|N|137|20|48|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine}}| |祭神 = Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto, Miho Susumi no Mikoto, Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto, and 3 others |社格 = Shikinaisha, Prefectural Shrine |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}} ({{ill|lt=97 BCE|Q41672|WD=Q41672}}-{{ill|lt=29 BCE|Q40835|WD=Q40835}}) |本殿 = nagare-zukuri |別名 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=September 14|Q2847|WD=Q2847}} |神事 = }} {{神社 |名称 = Suzu Shrine Okumiya |画像 = [[ファイル:Suzujinjaokumiyairiguchi.JPG|250px|Okumiya entrance]]
Okumiya entrance |所在地 = Noro-machi Ka-74, Suzu{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ |title=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|30|42.1|N|137|19|48.5|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine Okumiya}}| |祭神 = Mihosusumi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Futsunushi|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = |創建 = (trad.) reign of Emperor Sujin |本殿 = |別名 = Suzugatake Oku Shrine, Suzuoku Daimyojin |例祭 = |神事 = }} }} '''Suzu Shrine''' (すずじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Suzu|Q846345|WD=Q846345}}, {{ill|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281|WD=Q131281}}. '''Takakura-gu''' and '''Kinbun-gu''' are in {{ill|lt=Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|WD=Q11355314|lt=Misaki-cho Jike}}, Suzu, and the '''Okumiya''' is in Noro-machi, Suzu. === Overview === It is located in the northeastern part of Suzu City. The enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}, Miho Susumi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and three others. Near Takakura-gu are Suzu Shrine Okumiya and Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu. It holds cultural properties including a wooden male deity statue designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}. On {{ill|lt=April 24, 2015|Q2529|WD=Q2529}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 27), it was certified as a {{ill|lt=Japan Heritage|Q18382798|WD=Q18382798}} component under "Lanterns Dancing on the Peninsula: Noto - Enthralling Kiriko Festivals." {{maplink2 |frame=yes |zoom=12 |frame-lat=37.48 |frame-long=137.31 |frame-width=300 |frame-height=400 |frame-align=left |type=point |marker=1 |title=Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu |coord={{Coord |37.4982134 |137.3461987 }} |type2=point |marker2=2 |title2=Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu |coord2={{Coord |37.495727 |137.3445761 }} |type3=point |marker3=3 |title3=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |coord3={{Coord |37.5116835 |137.3301375 }} |type4=point |marker4=bus |title4=Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) |coord4={{Coord |37.4414395 |137.2701412 }} |text=(1) Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu
(2) Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu
(3) Suzu Shrine Okumiya
Lower left: Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) }} {{-|left}} === History === Shrine tradition says it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}} ({{ill|lt=97 BCE|Q41672|WD=Q41672}}-{{ill|lt=29 BCE|Q40835|WD=Q40835}}), and moved to the current place in the {{ill|lt=Tenpyo-shoho|Q1373169|WD=Q1373169}} era ({{ill|lt=749|Q23624|WD=Q23624}}-{{ill|lt=756|Q31121|WD=Q31121}}). In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small government-offering shrine. Under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine. There are three torii in the precinct, but in the {{ill|lt=2023|Q49622|WD=Q49622}} Okunoto earthquake of May, the first torii (about 9 m tall) collapsed and removal work began in August{{Cite web|和書|title=Removal begins at public expense for collapsed torii at Suzu Shrine|url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/articles/-/1155732 |website=Hokkoku Shimbun |date=2023-08-18 |access-date=2023-08-18 |language=ja}}. In the {{ill|lt=2024|Q49619|WD=Q49619}} {{ill|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake (2024)|Q124060919|WD=Q124060919|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake}}, the second torii collapsed due to quake and tsunami effects, and the third torii developed cracks{{Cite web |title=Suzu's Suzu Shrine heavily damaged again after last year; assistant priest Toyonobu Sarume: "Only rebuilding remains"|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/general/nikkan/news/202401210000971.html |website=Nikkan Sports|date=2024-01-21 |access-date=2024-01-21 |language=ja}}. In the same quake, the temizuya was completely destroyed, doors of the main hall were warped, and stone lanterns and guardian dogs were also damaged. === Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property * Five seated wooden male deity statues ; Natural Monument * Suzu Shrine sacred grove File:須須神社 - panoramio.jpg|Suzu Shrine sacred grove === Access === * By car : From {{ill|lt=Noto Satoyama Kaido|Q11612031|WD=Q11612031}}{{ill|lt=Anamizu Interchange|Q11597233|WD=Q11597233|lt=Anamizu I.C.}}, go east via {{ill|lt=Suzu Road|Q11573344|WD=Q11573344}}, then from the Rice Center-mae intersection take Prefectural Route 287 for about 1 hour. * Public transport : {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Suzu Bus|UNKNOWN}}, Noro-Iida (sea) route bound for "Noro," get off at "Suzu Jinja-mae"{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.suzu.lg.jp/site/kankou/1530.html |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Suzu City |date=2022-01-17 |accessdate=2024-01-12 }}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === {{参照方法|section=1|date=2024-01}} * Precinct signboard "Suzu Shrine <>" (no author/date listed) * ''Ishikawa Prefecture Shrine Record,'' edited and published by Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines (Oct 26, 1976) * ''Kano Local Terms,'' by Ken Hiki (published by {{ill|lt=Hokkoku Shimbun|Q11401312|WD=Q11401312|lt=Hokkoku Shimbun}} on Aug 1, 1956) === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|lt=Noto Province|Q1047671|WD=Q1047671}} * {{ill|lt=Okunoto|Q11446096|WD=Q11446096}} * {{ill|lt=Suzu Cape|Q11610957|WD=Q11610957}} * {{ill|lt=Suzu District|Q7651143|WD=Q7651143}} * {{ill|lt=Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|WD=Q11355314}} * {{ill|lt=Heki Village (Ishikawa)|Q17174281|WD=Q17174281}} * {{ill|lt=Saikai Village (Suzu District, Ishikawa)|Q11629087|WD=Q11629087}} === External links === * [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ Suzu Shrine] / [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ Suzu Shrine Okumiya] - The enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}, Miho Susumi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and three others. In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small government-offering shrine. There are three torii in the precinct, but in the {{ill|lt=2023|Q49622|WD=Q49622}} Okunoto earthquake of May, the first torii (about 9 m tall) collapsed and removal work began in August{{Cite web|和書|title=Removal begins at public expense for collapsed torii at Suzu Shrine|url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/articles/-/1155732 |website=Hokkoku Shimbun |date=2023-08-18 |access-date=2023-08-18 |language=ja}}. In the {{ill|lt=2024|Q49619|WD=Q49619}} {{ill|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake (2024)|Q124060919|WD=Q124060919|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake}}, the second torii collapsed due to quake and tsunami effects, and the third torii developed cracks{{Cite web |title=Suzu's Suzu Shrine heavily damaged again after last year; assistant priest Toyonobu Sarume: "Only rebuilding remains"|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/general/nikkan/news/202401210000971.html |website=Nikkan Sports|date=2024-01-21 |access-date=2024-01-21 |language=ja}}. In the same quake, the temizuya was completely destroyed, doors of the main hall were warped, and stone lanterns and guardian dogs were also damaged. === Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property * Five seated wooden male deity statues ; Natural Monument * Suzu Shrine sacred grove File:須須神社 - Takakura-gu main hall |所在地 = Misaki-cho Jike 4-2, Suzu, Ishikawa{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|29|54|N|137|20|48|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine}}| |祭神 = Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto, Miho Susumi no Mikoto, Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto, and 3 others |社格 = Shikinaisha, Prefectural Shrine |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}} ({{ill|lt=97 BCE|Q41672|WD=Q41672}}-{{ill|lt=29 BCE|Q40835|WD=Q40835}}) |本殿 = nagare-zukuri |別名 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=September 14|Q2847|WD=Q2847}} |神事 = }} {{神社 |名称 = Suzu Shrine Okumiya |画像 = [[ファイル:Suzujinjaokumiyairiguchi.JPG|250px|Okumiya entrance]]
Okumiya entrance |所在地 = Noro-machi Ka-74, Suzu{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ |title=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|30|42.1|N|137|19|48.5|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine Okumiya}}| |祭神 = Mihosusumi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = |創建 = (trad.) reign of Emperor Sujin |本殿 = |別名 = Suzugatake Oku Shrine, Suzuoku Daimyojin |例祭 = |神事 = }} }} '''Suzu Shrine''' (すずじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Suzu|Q846345|WD=Q846345}}, {{ill|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281|WD=Q131281}}. '''Takakura-gu''' and '''Kinbun-gu''' are in {{ill|lt=Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|WD=Q11355314|lt=Misaki-cho Jike}}, Suzu, and the '''Okumiya''' is in Noro-machi, Suzu. === Overview === It is located in the northeastern part of Suzu City. The enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}, Miho Susumi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and three others. Near Takakura-gu are Suzu Shrine Okumiya and Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu. It holds cultural properties including a wooden male deity statue designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}. On {{ill|lt=April 24, 2015|Q2529|WD=Q2529}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 27), it was certified as a {{ill|lt=Japan Heritage|Q18382798|WD=Q18382798}} component under "Lanterns Dancing on the Peninsula: Noto - Enthralling Kiriko Festivals." {{maplink2 |frame=yes |zoom=12 |frame-lat=37.48 |frame-long=137.31 |frame-width=300 |frame-height=400 |frame-align=left |type=point |marker=1 |title=Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu |coord={{Coord |37.4982134 |137.3461987 }} |type2=point |marker2=2 |title2=Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu |coord2={{Coord |37.495727 |137.3445761 }} |type3=point |marker3=3 |title3=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |coord3={{Coord |37.5116835 |137.3301375 }} |type4=point |marker4=bus |title4=Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) |coord4={{Coord |37.4414395 |137.2701412 }} |text=(1) Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu
(2) Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu
(3) Suzu Shrine Okumiya
Lower left: Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) }} {{-|left}} === History === Shrine tradition says it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}} ({{ill|lt=97 BCE|Q41672|WD=Q41672}}-{{ill|lt=29 BCE|Q40835|WD=Q40835}}), and moved to the current place in the {{ill|lt=Tenpyo-shoho|Q1373169|WD=Q1373169}} era ({{ill|lt=749|Q23624|WD=Q23624}}-{{ill|lt=756|Q31121|WD=Q31121}}). In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small government-offering shrine. Under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine. There are three torii in the precinct, but in the {{ill|lt=2023|Q49622|WD=Q49622}} Okunoto earthquake of May, the first torii (about 9 m tall) collapsed and removal work began in August{{Cite web|和書|title=Removal begins at public expense for collapsed torii at Suzu Shrine|url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/articles/-/1155732 |website=Hokkoku Shimbun |date=2023-08-18 |access-date=2023-08-18 |language=ja}}. In the {{ill|lt=2024|Q49619|WD=Q49619}} {{ill|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake (2024)|Q124060919|WD=Q124060919|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake}}, the second torii collapsed due to quake and tsunami effects, and the third torii developed cracks{{Cite web |title=Suzu's Suzu Shrine heavily damaged again after last year; assistant priest Toyonobu Sarume: "Only rebuilding remains"|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/general/nikkan/news/202401210000971.html |website=Nikkan Sports|date=2024-01-21 |access-date=2024-01-21 |language=ja}}. In the same quake, the temizuya was completely destroyed, doors of the main hall were warped, and stone lanterns and guardian dogs were also damaged. === Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property * Five seated wooden male deity statues ; Natural Monument * Suzu Shrine sacred grove File:須須神社 - panoramio.jpg|Suzu Shrine sacred grove === Access === * By car : From {{ill|lt=Noto Satoyama Kaido|Q11612031|WD=Q11612031}}{{ill|lt=Anamizu Interchange|Q11597233|WD=Q11597233|lt=Anamizu I.C.}}, go east via {{ill|lt=Suzu Road|Q11573344|WD=Q11573344}}, then from the Rice Center-mae intersection take Prefectural Route 287 for about 1 hour. * Public transport : {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Suzu Bus|1=UNKNOWN}}, Noro-Iida (sea) route bound for "Noro," get off at "Suzu Jinja-mae"{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.suzu.lg.jp/site/kankou/1530.html |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Suzu City |date=2022-01-17 |accessdate=2024-01-12 }}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === {{参照方法|section=1|date=2024-01}} * Precinct signboard "Suzu Shrine <>" (no author/date listed) * ''Ishikawa Prefecture Shrine Record,'' edited and published by Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines (Oct 26, 1976) * ''Kano Local Terms,'' by Ken Hiki (published by {{ill|lt=Hokkoku Shimbun|Q11401312|WD=Q11401312|lt=Hokkoku Shimbun}} on Aug 1, 1956) === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|lt=Noto Province|Q1047671|WD=Q1047671}} * {{ill|lt=Okunoto|Q11446096|WD=Q11446096}} * {{ill|lt=Suzu Cape|Q11610957|WD=Q11610957}} * {{ill|lt=Suzu District|Q7651143|WD=Q7651143}} * {{ill|lt=Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|WD=Q11355314}} * {{ill|lt=Heki Village (Ishikawa)|Q17174281|WD=Q17174281}} * {{ill|lt=Saikai Village (Suzu District, Ishikawa)|Q11629087|WD=Q11629087}} === External links === * [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ Suzu Shrine] / [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ Suzu Shrine Okumiya] - Takakura-gu main hall |所在地 = Misaki-cho Jike 4-2, Suzu, Ishikawa{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|29|54|N|137|20|48|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine}}| |祭神 = Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto, Miho Susumi no Mikoto, Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto, and 3 others |社格 = Shikinaisha, Prefectural Shrine |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}} ({{ill|lt=97 BCE|Q41672|WD=Q41672}}-{{ill|lt=29 BCE|Q40835|WD=Q40835}}) |本殿 = nagare-zukuri |別名 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=September 14|Q2847|WD=Q2847}} |神事 = }} {{神社 |名称 = Suzu Shrine Okumiya |画像 = [[ファイル:Suzujinjaokumiyairiguchi.JPG|250px|Okumiya entrance]]
Okumiya entrance |所在地 = Noro-machi Ka-74, Suzu{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ |title=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|30|42.1|N|137|19|48.5|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine Okumiya}}| |祭神 = Mihosusumi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = |創建 = (trad.) reign of Emperor Sujin |本殿 = |別名 = Suzugatake Oku Shrine, Suzuoku Daimyojin |例祭 = |神事 = }} }} '''Suzu Shrine''' (すずじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Suzu|Q846345|WD=Q846345}}, {{ill|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281|WD=Q131281}}. '''Takakura-gu''' and '''Kinbun-gu''' are in {{ill|lt=Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|WD=Q11355314|lt=Misaki-cho Jike}}, Suzu, and the '''Okumiya''' is in Noro-machi, Suzu. === Overview === It is located in the northeastern part of Suzu City. The enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}, Miho Susumi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and three others. Near Takakura-gu are Suzu Shrine Okumiya and Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu. It holds cultural properties including a wooden male deity statue designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}. On {{ill|lt=April 24, 2015|Q2529|WD=Q2529}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 27), it was certified as a {{ill|lt=Japan Heritage|Q18382798|WD=Q18382798}} component under "Lanterns Dancing on the Peninsula: Noto - Enthralling Kiriko Festivals." {{maplink2 |frame=yes |zoom=12 |frame-lat=37.48 |frame-long=137.31 |frame-width=300 |frame-height=400 |frame-align=left |type=point |marker=1 |title=Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu |coord={{Coord |37.4982134 |137.3461987 }} |type2=point |marker2=2 |title2=Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu |coord2={{Coord |37.495727 |137.3445761 }} |type3=point |marker3=3 |title3=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |coord3={{Coord |37.5116835 |137.3301375 }} |type4=point |marker4=bus |title4=Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) |coord4={{Coord |37.4414395 |137.2701412 }} |text=(1) Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu
(2) Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu
(3) Suzu Shrine Okumiya
Lower left: Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) }} {{-|left}} === History === Shrine tradition says it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}} ({{ill|lt=97 BCE|Q41672|WD=Q41672}}-{{ill|lt=29 BCE|Q40835|WD=Q40835}}), and moved to the current place in the {{ill|lt=Tenpyo-shoho|Q1373169|WD=Q1373169}} era ({{ill|lt=749|Q23624|WD=Q23624}}-{{ill|lt=756|Q31121|WD=Q31121}}). In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small government-offering shrine. Under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine. There are three torii in the precinct, but in the {{ill|lt=2023|Q49622|WD=Q49622}} Okunoto earthquake of May, the first torii (about 9 m tall) collapsed and removal work began in August{{Cite web|和書|title=Removal begins at public expense for collapsed torii at Suzu Shrine|url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/articles/-/1155732 |website=Hokkoku Shimbun |date=2023-08-18 |access-date=2023-08-18 |language=ja}}. In the {{ill|lt=2024|Q49619|WD=Q49619}} {{ill|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake (2024)|Q124060919|WD=Q124060919|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake}}, the second torii collapsed due to quake and tsunami effects, and the third torii developed cracks{{Cite web |title=Suzu's Suzu Shrine heavily damaged again after last year; assistant priest Toyonobu Sarume: "Only rebuilding remains"|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/general/nikkan/news/202401210000971.html |website=Nikkan Sports|date=2024-01-21 |access-date=2024-01-21 |language=ja}}. In the same quake, the temizuya was completely destroyed, doors of the main hall were warped, and stone lanterns and guardian dogs were also damaged. === Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property * Five seated wooden male deity statues ; Natural Monument * Suzu Shrine sacred grove File:須須神社 - panoramio.jpg|Suzu Shrine sacred grove === Access === * By car : From {{ill|lt=Noto Satoyama Kaido|Q11612031|WD=Q11612031}}{{ill|lt=Anamizu Interchange|Q11597233|WD=Q11597233|lt=Anamizu I.C.}}, go east via {{ill|lt=Suzu Road|Q11573344|WD=Q11573344}}, then from the Rice Center-mae intersection take Prefectural Route 287 for about 1 hour. * Public transport : {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Suzu Bus|1=UNKNOWN}}, Noro-Iida (sea) route bound for "Noro," get off at "Suzu Jinja-mae"{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.suzu.lg.jp/site/kankou/1530.html |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Suzu City |date=2022-01-17 |accessdate=2024-01-12 }}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === {{参照方法|section=1|date=2024-01}} * Precinct signboard "Suzu Shrine <>" (no author/date listed) * ''Ishikawa Prefecture Shrine Record,'' edited and published by Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines (Oct 26, 1976) * ''Kano Local Terms,'' by Ken Hiki (published by {{ill|lt=Hokkoku Shimbun|Q11401312|WD=Q11401312|lt=Hokkoku Shimbun}} on Aug 1, 1956) === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|lt=Noto Province|Q1047671|WD=Q1047671}} * {{ill|lt=Okunoto|Q11446096|WD=Q11446096}} * {{ill|lt=Suzu Cape|Q11610957|WD=Q11610957}} * {{ill|lt=Suzu District|Q7651143|WD=Q7651143}} * {{ill|lt=Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|WD=Q11355314}} * {{ill|lt=Heki Village (Ishikawa)|Q17174281|WD=Q17174281}} * {{ill|lt=Saikai Village (Suzu District, Ishikawa)|Q11629087|WD=Q11629087}} === External links === * [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ Suzu Shrine] / [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ Suzu Shrine Okumiya] - '''Takakura-gu''' and '''Kinbun-gu''' are in {{ill|lt=Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|WD=Q11355314|lt=Misaki-cho Jike}}, Suzu, and the '''Okumiya''' is in Noro-machi, Suzu. === Overview === It is located in the northeastern part of Suzu City. The enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}, Miho Susumi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}}, and three others. Near Takakura-gu are Suzu Shrine Okumiya and Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu. It holds cultural properties including a wooden male deity statue designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}. On {{ill|lt=April 24, 2015|Q2529|WD=Q2529}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 27), it was certified as a {{ill|lt=Japan Heritage|Q18382798|WD=Q18382798}} component under "Lanterns Dancing on the Peninsula: Noto - Enthralling Kiriko Festivals." {{maplink2 |frame=yes |zoom=12 |frame-lat=37.48 |frame-long=137.31 |frame-width=300 |frame-height=400 |frame-align=left |type=point |marker=1 |title=Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu |coord={{Coord |37.4982134 |137.3461987 }} |type2=point |marker2=2 |title2=Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu |coord2={{Coord |37.495727 |137.3445761 }} |type3=point |marker3=3 |title3=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |coord3={{Coord |37.5116835 |137.3301375 }} |type4=point |marker4=bus |title4=Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) |coord4={{Coord |37.4414395 |137.2701412 }} |text=(1) Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu

Lower left: Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) }} {{-|left}} === History === Shrine tradition says it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530}} ({{ill|lt=97 BCE|Q41672|WD=Q41672}}-{{ill|lt=29 BCE|Q40835|WD=Q40835}}), and moved to the current place in the {{ill|lt=Tenpyo-shoho|Q1373169|WD=Q1373169}} era ({{ill|lt=749|Q23624|WD=Q23624}}-{{ill|lt=756|Q31121|WD=Q31121}}). In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, it is listed as a small government-offering shrine. Under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine. There are three torii in the precinct, but in the {{ill|lt=2023|Q49622|WD=Q49622}} Okunoto earthquake of May, the first torii (about 9 m tall) collapsed and removal work began in August{{Cite web|和書|title=Removal begins at public expense for collapsed torii at Suzu Shrine|url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/articles/-/1155732 |website=Hokkoku Shimbun |date=2023-08-18 |access-date=2023-08-18 |language=ja}}. In the {{ill|lt=2024|Q49619|WD=Q49619}} {{ill|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake (2024)|Q124060919|WD=Q124060919|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake}}, the second torii collapsed due to quake and tsunami effects, and the third torii developed cracks{{Cite web |title=Suzu's Suzu Shrine heavily damaged again after last year; assistant priest Toyonobu Sarume: "Only rebuilding remains"|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/general/nikkan/news/202401210000971.html |website=Nikkan Sports|date=2024-01-21 |access-date=2024-01-21 |language=ja}}. In the same quake, the temizuya was completely destroyed, doors of the main hall were warped, and stone lanterns and guardian dogs were also damaged. === Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property * Five seated wooden male deity statues ; Natural Monument * Suzu Shrine sacred grove File:須須神社 - Suzujinjaokumiyairiguchi.JPG {{commons category|Category:Suzu-jinja}} [[JA:須須神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11664846}} == Japanese content == {{右| {{神社 |名称 = Suzu Shrine |画像 = [[ファイル:Suzujinjahonden.JPG|250px|Takakura-gu main hall]]
Takakura-gu main hall |所在地 = Misaki-cho Jike 4-2, Suzu, Ishikawa{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|29|54|N|137|20|48|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine}}| |祭神 = Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto, Miho Susumi no Mikoto, Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto, and 3 others |社格 = Shikinaisha, Prefectural Shrine |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}} ({{ill|lt=97 BCE|Q41672|WD=Q41672|1=97 BC}}-{{ill|lt=29 BCE|Q40835|WD=Q40835|1=29 BC}}) |本殿 = nagare-zukuri |別名 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=September 14|Q2847|WD=Q2847|1=September 14}} |神事 = }} {{神社 |名称 = Suzu Shrine Okumiya |画像 = [[ファイル:Suzujinjaokumiyairiguchi.JPG|250px|Okumiya entrance]]
Okumiya entrance |所在地 = Noro-machi Ka-74, Suzu{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ |title=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|30|42.1|N|137|19|48.5|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine Okumiya}}| |祭神 = Mihosusumi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}}, {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=Futsunushi}} |社格 = |創建 = (trad.) reign of Emperor Sujin |本殿 = |別名 = Suzugatake Oku Shrine, Suzuoku Daimyojin |例祭 = |神事 = }} }} '''Suzu Shrine''' (すずじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Suzu|Q846345|WD=Q846345|1=Suzu, Ishikawa}}, {{ill|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281|WD=Q131281|1=Ishikawa Prefecture}}. '''Takakura-gu''' and '''Kinbun-gu''' are in {{ill|lt=Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|WD=Q11355314|lt=Misaki-cho Jike|1=Misaki}}, Suzu, and the '''Okumiya''' is in Noro-machi, Suzu. === Overview === It is located in the northeastern part of Suzu City. The enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}, Miho Susumi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}}, and three others. Near Takakura-gu are Suzu Shrine Okumiya and Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu. It holds cultural properties including a wooden male deity statue designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. On {{ill|lt=April 24, 2015|Q2529|WD=Q2529|1=April 24}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 27), it was certified as a {{ill|lt=Japan Heritage|Q18382798|WD=Q18382798|1=Japan Heritage}} component under "Lanterns Dancing on the Peninsula: Noto - Enthralling Kiriko Festivals." {{maplink2 |frame=yes |zoom=12 |frame-lat=37.48 |frame-long=137.31 |frame-width=300 |frame-height=400 |frame-align=left |type=point |marker=1 |title=Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu |coord={{Coord |37.4982134 |137.3461987 }} |type2=point |marker2=2 |title2=Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu |coord2={{Coord |37.495727 |137.3445761 }} |type3=point |marker3=3 |title3=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |coord3={{Coord |37.5116835 |137.3301375 }} |type4=point |marker4=bus |title4=Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) |coord4={{Coord |37.4414395 |137.2701412 }} |text=(1) Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu
(2) Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu
(3) Suzu Shrine Okumiya
Lower left: Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) }} {{-|left}} === History === Shrine tradition says it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}} ({{ill|lt=97 BCE|Q41672|WD=Q41672|1=97 BC}}-{{ill|lt=29 BCE|Q40835|WD=Q40835|1=29 BC}}), and moved to the current place in the {{ill|lt=Tenpyo-shoho|Q1373169|WD=Q1373169|1=Tenpyō-shōhō}} era ({{ill|lt=749|Q23624|WD=Q23624|1=749}}-{{ill|lt=756|Q31121|WD=Q31121|1=756}}). In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it is listed as a small government-offering shrine. Under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine. There are three torii in the precinct, but in the {{ill|lt=2023|Q49622|WD=Q49622|1=2023}} Okunoto earthquake of May, the first torii (about 9 m tall) collapsed and removal work began in August{{Cite web|和書|title=Removal begins at public expense for collapsed torii at Suzu Shrine|url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/articles/-/1155732 |website=Hokkoku Shimbun |date=2023-08-18 |access-date=2023-08-18 |language=ja}}. In the {{ill|lt=2024|Q49619|WD=Q49619|1=2024}} {{ill|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake (2024)|Q124060919|WD=Q124060919|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake|1=2024 Noto earthquake}}, the second torii collapsed due to quake and tsunami effects, and the third torii developed cracks{{Cite web |title=Suzu's Suzu Shrine heavily damaged again after last year; assistant priest Toyonobu Sarume: "Only rebuilding remains"|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/general/nikkan/news/202401210000971.html |website=Nikkan Sports|date=2024-01-21 |access-date=2024-01-21 |language=ja}}. In the same quake, the temizuya was completely destroyed, doors of the main hall were warped, and stone lanterns and guardian dogs were also damaged. === Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property * Five seated wooden male deity statues ; Natural Monument * Suzu Shrine sacred grove File:須須神社 - panoramio.jpg|Suzu Shrine sacred grove === Access === * By car : From {{ill|lt=Noto Satoyama Kaido|Q11612031|WD=Q11612031|1=Noto-Satoyama Kaidō}}{{ill|lt=Anamizu Interchange|Q11597233|WD=Q11597233|lt=Anamizu I.C.|1=Anamizu Interchange}}, go east via {{ill|lt=Suzu Road|Q11573344|WD=Q11573344|1=Suzu Road}}, then from the Rice Center-mae intersection take Prefectural Route 287 for about 1 hour. * Public transport : {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Suzu Bus|1=UNKNOWN}}, Noro-Iida (sea) route bound for "Noro," get off at "Suzu Jinja-mae"{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.suzu.lg.jp/site/kankou/1530.html |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Suzu City |date=2022-01-17 |accessdate=2024-01-12 }}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === {{参照方法|section=1|date=2024-01}} * Precinct signboard "Suzu Shrine <>" (no author/date listed) * ''Ishikawa Prefecture Shrine Record,'' edited and published by Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines (Oct 26, 1976) * ''Kano Local Terms,'' by Ken Hiki (published by {{ill|lt=Hokkoku Shimbun|Q11401312|WD=Q11401312|lt=Hokkoku Shimbun|1=Hokkoku Shimbun}} on Aug 1, 1956) === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|lt=Noto Province|Q1047671|WD=Q1047671|1=Noto Province}} * {{ill|lt=Okunoto|Q11446096|WD=Q11446096|1=Okunoto}} * {{ill|lt=Suzu Cape|Q11610957|WD=Q11610957|1=Cape Suzu}} * {{ill|lt=Suzu District|Q7651143|WD=Q7651143|1=Suzu District, Ishikawa}} * {{ill|lt=Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|WD=Q11355314|1=Misaki}} * {{ill|lt=Heki Village (Ishikawa)|Q17174281|WD=Q17174281|1=Hiki}} * {{ill|lt=Saikai Village (Suzu District, Ishikawa)|Q11629087|WD=Q11629087|1=Saikai}} === External links === * [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ Suzu Shrine] / [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ Suzu Shrine Okumiya] - Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines * [https://www.city.suzu.lg.jp/kankounavi/spot/history/temple/suzu_shrine.html Suzu Shrine] - Suzu Tourism Navi Net (Suzu City) * [http://www.pref.ishikawa.lg.jp/kyoiku/bunkazai/siseki/3-5.html#susu Suzu Shrine Sacred Grove] - [[ファイル:Suzujinjahonden.JPG|250px|Takakura-gu main hall]]
Takakura-gu main hall |所在地 = Misaki-cho Jike 4-2, Suzu, Ishikawa{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|29|54|N|137|20|48|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine}}| |祭神 = Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto, Miho Susumi no Mikoto, Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto, and 3 others |社格 = Shikinaisha, Prefectural Shrine |創建 = (trad.) reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}} ({{ill|lt=97 BCE|Q41672|WD=Q41672|1=97 BC}}-{{ill|lt=29 BCE|Q40835|WD=Q40835|1=29 BC}}) |本殿 = nagare-zukuri |別名 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=September 14|Q2847|WD=Q2847|1=September 14}} |神事 = }} {{神社 |名称 = Suzu Shrine Okumiya |画像 = [[ファイル:Suzujinjaokumiyairiguchi.JPG|250px|Okumiya entrance]]
Okumiya entrance |所在地 = Noro-machi Ka-74, Suzu{{Cite web |url=https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ |title=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines |accessdate=2024-01-12 }} |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|37|30|42.1|N|137|19|48.5|E|region:JP-17|name=Suzu Shrine Okumiya}}| |祭神 = Mihosusumi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}}, {{ill|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi|Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|WD=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=Futsunushi}} |社格 = |創建 = (trad.) reign of Emperor Sujin |本殿 = |別名 = Suzugatake Oku Shrine, Suzuoku Daimyojin |例祭 = |神事 = }} }} '''Suzu Shrine''' (すずじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Suzu|Q846345|WD=Q846345|1=Suzu, Ishikawa}}, {{ill|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281|WD=Q131281|1=Ishikawa Prefecture}}. '''Takakura-gu''' and '''Kinbun-gu''' are in {{ill|lt=Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|WD=Q11355314|lt=Misaki-cho Jike|1=Misaki}}, Suzu, and the '''Okumiya''' is in Noro-machi, Suzu. === Overview === It is located in the northeastern part of Suzu City. The enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Ninigi|Q1056017|WD=Q1056017|lt=Amatsu-Hitaka-Hiko-Ho-no-Ninigi-no-Mikoto|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}, Miho Susumi-no-Mikoto, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}}, and three others. Near Takakura-gu are Suzu Shrine Okumiya and Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu. It holds cultural properties including a wooden male deity statue designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. On {{ill|lt=April 24, 2015|Q2529|WD=Q2529|1=April 24}} ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 27), it was certified as a {{ill|lt=Japan Heritage|Q18382798|WD=Q18382798|1=Japan Heritage}} component under "Lanterns Dancing on the Peninsula: Noto - Enthralling Kiriko Festivals." {{maplink2 |frame=yes |zoom=12 |frame-lat=37.48 |frame-long=137.31 |frame-width=300 |frame-height=400 |frame-align=left |type=point |marker=1 |title=Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu |coord={{Coord |37.4982134 |137.3461987 }} |type2=point |marker2=2 |title2=Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu |coord2={{Coord |37.495727 |137.3445761 }} |type3=point |marker3=3 |title3=Suzu Shrine Okumiya |coord3={{Coord |37.5116835 |137.3301375 }} |type4=point |marker4=bus |title4=Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) |coord4={{Coord |37.4414395 |137.2701412 }} |text=(1) Suzu Shrine Takakura-gu
(2) Suzu Shrine Kinbun-gu
(3) Suzu Shrine Okumiya
Lower left: Roadside Station Suzunari (Suzunari-kan) }} {{-|left}} === History === Shrine tradition says it was founded in the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Sujin|Q333530|WD=Q333530|1=Emperor Sujin}} ({{ill|lt=97 BCE|Q41672|WD=Q41672|1=97 BC}}-{{ill|lt=29 BCE|Q40835|WD=Q40835|1=29 BC}}), and moved to the current place in the {{ill|lt=Tenpyo-shoho|Q1373169|WD=Q1373169|1=Tenpyō-shōhō}} era ({{ill|lt=749|Q23624|WD=Q23624|1=749}}-{{ill|lt=756|Q31121|WD=Q31121|1=756}}). In the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, it is listed as a small government-offering shrine. Under the {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine. There are three torii in the precinct, but in the {{ill|lt=2023|Q49622|WD=Q49622|1=2023}} Okunoto earthquake of May, the first torii (about 9 m tall) collapsed and removal work began in August{{Cite web|和書|title=Removal begins at public expense for collapsed torii at Suzu Shrine|url=https://www.hokkoku.co.jp/articles/-/1155732 |website=Hokkoku Shimbun |date=2023-08-18 |access-date=2023-08-18 |language=ja}}. In the {{ill|lt=2024|Q49619|WD=Q49619|1=2024}} {{ill|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake (2024)|Q124060919|WD=Q124060919|lt=Noto Peninsula earthquake|1=2024 Noto earthquake}}, the second torii collapsed due to quake and tsunami effects, and the third torii developed cracks{{Cite web |title=Suzu's Suzu Shrine heavily damaged again after last year; assistant priest Toyonobu Sarume: "Only rebuilding remains"|url=https://www.nikkansports.com/general/nikkan/news/202401210000971.html |website=Nikkan Sports|date=2024-01-21 |access-date=2024-01-21 |language=ja}}. In the same quake, the temizuya was completely destroyed, doors of the main hall were warped, and stone lanterns and guardian dogs were also damaged. === Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property * Five seated wooden male deity statues ; Natural Monument * Suzu Shrine sacred grove File:須須神社 - panoramio.jpg|Suzu Shrine sacred grove === Access === * By car : From {{ill|lt=Noto Satoyama Kaido|Q11612031|WD=Q11612031|1=Noto-Satoyama Kaidō}}{{ill|lt=Anamizu Interchange|Q11597233|WD=Q11597233|lt=Anamizu I.C.|1=Anamizu Interchange}}, go east via {{ill|lt=Suzu Road|Q11573344|WD=Q11573344|1=Suzu Road}}, then from the Rice Center-mae intersection take Prefectural Route 287 for about 1 hour. * Public transport : {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Suzu Bus|1=UNKNOWN}}, Noro-Iida (sea) route bound for "Noro," get off at "Suzu Jinja-mae"{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.suzu.lg.jp/site/kankou/1530.html |title=Suzu Shrine |publisher=Suzu City |date=2022-01-17 |accessdate=2024-01-12 }}. === Footnotes === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === {{参照方法|section=1|date=2024-01}} * Precinct signboard "Suzu Shrine <>" (no author/date listed) * ''Ishikawa Prefecture Shrine Record,'' edited and published by Ishikawa Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines (Oct 26, 1976) * ''Kano Local Terms,'' by Ken Hiki (published by {{ill|lt=Hokkoku Shimbun|Q11401312|WD=Q11401312|lt=Hokkoku Shimbun|1=Hokkoku Shimbun}} on Aug 1, 1956) === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|lt=Noto Province|Q1047671|WD=Q1047671|1=Noto Province}} * {{ill|lt=Okunoto|Q11446096|WD=Q11446096|1=Okunoto}} * {{ill|lt=Suzu Cape|Q11610957|WD=Q11610957|1=Cape Suzu}} * {{ill|lt=Suzu District|Q7651143|WD=Q7651143|1=Suzu District, Ishikawa}} * {{ill|lt=Misaki Village (Ishikawa)|Q11355314|WD=Q11355314|1=Misaki}} * {{ill|lt=Heki Village (Ishikawa)|Q17174281|WD=Q17174281|1=Hiki}} * {{ill|lt=Saikai Village (Suzu District, Ishikawa)|Q11629087|WD=Q11629087|1=Saikai}} === External links === * [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1827/ Suzu Shrine] / [https://www.ishikawa-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/j1834/ Suzu Shrine Okumiya] -
